skill.name: entra-app-registration
skill.description: Guides Microsoft Entra ID app registration, OAuth 2.0 authentication setup, API permissions, service principals, and MSAL integration examples.
skill.summary: Configure Microsoft Entra app registrations, OAuth flows, permissions, and MSAL client integration.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, and prompt-injection indicators. Review found these are mostly documentation examples for Azure CLI, OAuth, Microsoft Graph, and SDK usage, not hidden executable logic. The skill is publishable with a warning because it guides privileged identity administration that can grant permissions, create credentials, or delete app registrations.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Entra Administration Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill includes Azure CLI examples that create app registrations, grant admin consent, create service principals, and delete app registrations. These are legitimate administrative tasks, but misuse can change tenant access and should require explicit user approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation examples and not covert execution. The operations are still sensitive because they alter identity objects and permissions.

medium_findings.title: Broad Microsoft Graph Permission Examples
medium_findings.description: The permission reference lists delegated and application permissions such as Directory.ReadWrite.All, Mail.Read, Mail.Send, and User.ReadWrite.All. This is legitimate documentation, but users could over-grant access if least privilege is not enforced.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed permissions are real high-impact Microsoft Graph scopes. The surrounding content describes permission configuration rather than malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Credential Creation and Handling Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill documents client secrets, certificate upload, and credential reset flows. This is expected for app registration guidance, but outputs and files can expose credentials if copied into insecure logs or repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Credential commands and secret-handling text are present in the referenced files. The guidance also recommends secure storage, so the risk is contextual rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many fenced shell commands and command substitutions as Ruby backtick or shell execution. Review found Azure CLI examples in Markdown, not code that the skill executes automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content appears inside Markdown command examples. No executable wrapper or automatic invocation was found in the reviewed skill files.

low_findings.title: Network URL Findings Are Expected Microsoft Identity Endpoints
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to Microsoft Graph, Microsoft login endpoints, localhost redirect URIs, example application URLs, and official documentation. No evidence found of unauthorized outbound exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs match the skill domain and are used for OAuth examples or documentation links. The examples do not send secrets to unrelated domains.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Finding Dismissed After Context Review
low_findings.description: The static prompt-extraction alert matched OAuth device-code text that tells a user how to sign in. No override commands, fake system messages, or instructions to skip review were found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are normal device-code authentication instructions. A targeted search found no prompt-injection language in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Mostly Identifier and Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged terms in OAuth, GUIDs, permission names, and app registration examples as weak cryptography. Review found no implementation of weak hash algorithms or custom cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled locations contain OAuth scope definitions, GUID values, and best-practice text. No MD5, SHA-1, or custom crypto operation was identified in context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Tenant-Modifying CLI Procedures
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include creating app registrations, adding permissions, granting admin consent, creating service principals, and deleting applications. Assistants should present these as reviewable commands and confirm tenant, app ID, and permission scope before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These commands directly modify Microsoft Entra state. The risk is operational misuse, not hidden malware.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Material Workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples cover creating secrets and uploading certificates. Assistants should avoid printing secret values in shared logs and should recommend managed identity or Key Vault where possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Credential handling appears as expected administrative guidance. The reviewed text includes secure-storage recommendations, lowering malicious-risk confidence.

content.value_statement: Microsoft Entra app registration setup is complex and easy to over-permission. This skill guides OAuth settings, API permissions, credentials, and MSAL examples with security context.
actual_capabilities: Explains Microsoft Entra app registration concepts and required identifiers.
Guides portal, Azure CLI, and Bicep setup paths for app registrations.
Maps OAuth flows to web, SPA, native, console, and service applications.
Provides Microsoft Graph permission examples with delegated and application scopes.
Includes MSAL console application examples for C#, Python, and JavaScript.
Documents troubleshooting steps for common Entra authentication errors.
limitations: Does not replace tenant administrator approval or change-control review.
Does not validate live Azure tenant policy or conditional access settings.
Permission IDs and platform behavior should be checked against current Microsoft documentation.
Examples require user-provided tenant IDs, client IDs, redirect URIs, and consent decisions.

use_cases.title: Create a first app registration
use_cases.description: Walk through tenant selection, redirect URI setup, client ID capture, and safe default permissions.

use_cases.title: Prepare production authentication
use_cases.description: Choose OAuth flow, credentials, redirect URIs, and Graph permissions for a service or web app.

use_cases.title: Debug sign-in and consent failures
use_cases.description: Diagnose redirect mismatch, invalid secret, token validation, and admin consent issues.

prompt_templates.title: Plan my registration
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a Microsoft Entra app registration for a web application. Ask for missing details before suggesting settings.

prompt_templates.title: Choose OAuth flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare OAuth flows for my SPA, API, and background service. Recommend the safest flow for each component.

prompt_templates.title: Review permissions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Microsoft Graph permissions for my app and suggest lower-privilege alternatives where possible.

prompt_templates.title: Generate an implementation checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production checklist for Entra app registration, consent, credential storage, token validation, and monitoring.

output_examples.input: I need a console app that signs in users and reads their profile.
output: Recommended app type: public client or native application.
Use authorization code or device code flow with MSAL.
Start with User.Read and avoid application permissions unless required.
Register localhost redirect URIs only for local development.

output_examples.input: My web app receives a redirect URI mismatch error.
output: Compare the request URI with the registered URI exactly.
Check scheme, host casing, path, port, and trailing slash.
Confirm the URI is configured under the correct platform type.
Update the app registration after change-control approval.

best_practices: Request the minimum Graph permissions needed for the app workflow.
Use managed identity or Key Vault instead of hardcoded secrets.
Confirm tenant, app ID, redirect URI, and consent impact before running commands.
anti_patterns: Granting broad application permissions before proving they are required.
Copying client secret values into prompts, logs, tickets, or source files.
Using production app registrations for local experiments or tutorials.

faq.question: Can this skill create resources automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and command examples. A user or administrator must run and approve any tenant changes.

faq.question: Does it support Azure AD terminology?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses Microsoft Entra ID and notes the former Azure Active Directory naming where helpful.

faq.question: Can it choose Microsoft Graph permissions?
faq.answer: It can suggest likely permissions and least-privilege alternatives, but administrators must approve the final consent.

faq.question: Does it handle secrets safely?
faq.answer: It recommends secure storage, Key Vault, managed identity, and careful handling of generated secret values.

faq.question: Can it troubleshoot OAuth errors?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers redirect URI mismatch, invalid client secrets, consent errors, token issues, and common configuration problems.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production tenants?
faq.answer: Yes, with change control. Review every command, permission, redirect URI, and credential decision before applying changes.
